nodes.intent: The platform recognizes a repeating operational pattern and eliminates it — no landing zone ever needs a human for that task again.
violations: implementing before reading all decisions
steps: Read all design decisions in decisions[] to understand access, identity, and image constraints before writing any code.
Implement the job logic under landing-zones/automation/ — jobs must use the ARM API only; no data-plane or VNet-dependent calls.
Add a Dockerfile for the new image — base image must be pullable from a public registry without authentication.
Register the new job in landing-zones/bicep/modules/landingzone-automation.bicep following the existing job definition pattern.
Verify the job authenticates as the landing zone identity — no separate identity or secret.
Present a complete draft of all changes before implementing.

nodes.intent: What was blocked is now validated and available to every landing zone at once.
violations: implementing before reading all decisions
steps: Read all design decisions in decisions[].
Add the resource type to allowedResources.json.
Use mcp__azure__documentation microsoft_docs_search to find the built-in diagnostic settings policy — add it to diagnosticSettings.bicepparam.
For each file in oases/, use mcp__azure__documentation microsoft_docs_search to find built-in policies with the required effect — add matching entries.
For each gap where no built-in policy provides the required effect, author a custom definition in customDefinitions/ and register it in policyDefinitions.bicepparam.
Check defenderForCloudExemptions.jsonc for paid-SKU recommendations specific to this resource type — add qualifying entries.
Check accessControl.bicepparam for custom role actions needed by this resource type.
Present a complete draft of all changes before implementing.

nodes.intent: The platform hands over an isolated environment — from this point, the application team operates independently.
violations: implementing before reading all decisions
steps: Confirm the application's file exists under join-the-platform/ — if not, the register-application-profile operation must be completed first.
Collect from the user: application name, environment (test or prod), management group name (oases-prod or oases-test), and budget (optional, default 100).
Auto-select the subscription ID: run `az account management-group subscription show-sub-under-mg --name subscription-bank --query "[0].name" -o tsv`.

Confirm the values with the user (including the auto-selected subscription ID), then trigger the workflow: gh workflow run requestNew-Landing-Zone.yml -f ApplicationName="{AppName}" -f Environment="{env}" -f ManagementGroupName="{mgmtGroup}" -f SubscriptionId="{subscriptionId}" -f Budget="{budget}" — this raises a pull request.
A platform engineer reviews and approves the pull request — approval is what triggers the automation to complete provisioning.

nodes.intent: The platform reclaims what it gave — the subscription returns to the Subscription Bank.
violations: implementing before reading all decisions
steps: Read all design decisions in decisions[] to understand what is being destroyed and what constraints apply.
Collect from the user: subscription ID of the landing zone to decommission.
Verify the bicepparam file exists at landing-zones/oases-prod/{app}/{env}.bicepparam and the lz workflow at .github/workflows/lz-oasis-{appName}-{env}.yml — do not proceed if either is missing.

Present to the user what will be permanently destroyed: deployment stack, resource groups, role assignments, budget, Defender settings. Confirm the subscription returns to the bank. Wait for explicit confirmation.

Trigger lz-flow-destroy-landing-zone via workflow_dispatch with the subscription ID. Do not manually delete files — the workflow resolves app name and env, creates the cleanup PR, and the merge triggers the Azure destroy sequence.

nodes.intent: The platform extends its reach to a new team — from this point, they provision and manage their own landing zones.
violations: implementing before reading all decisions
steps: Collect from the user: application name, owner email, and engineer email.

Confirm the values with the user, then trigger the workflow: gh workflow run requestNew-Join-The-Platform.yml -f ApplicationName="{AppName}" -f OwnerContact="{ownerEmail}" -f engineerContact="{engineerEmail}" — this raises a pull request.
A platform engineer reviews and approves the pull request — approval is what triggers the automation to complete provisioning.

nodes.intent: What was unclear becomes a clear picture — the more the graph grows, the fewer questions need a human to answer.
violations: writing a file before presenting a draft to the user
adding a bidirectional link
link note that does not answer 'how does this linked decision specifically affect or constrain this decision?'
violation that restates the decision instead of describing a detectable breach
steps: Identify whether this is a new entry or an update to an existing one. For an update, read the existing file first.
Determine the type: guiding-principles, decisions, or operations.

Draft the entry. For decisions: id matches filename; decision is a single clear statement; why explains the consequence of not following it; links are organic and unidirectional — each note answers 'how does this linked decision specifically affect or constrain this decision?'; violations are specific enough to detect in a code review; files list only files where a reviewer would look to verify the decision is respected.
For links: only add one if the note would change how someone implements or reviews this decision. Drop it if the relationship is obvious from context alone.
Present the complete draft to the user before writing any file.
Write the file.
Run the validation script: bash .claude/validate-knowledge-base.sh. Fix any errors before proceeding.
Open a PR.

nodes.intent: The application team reshapes their landing zone on demand — within the boundaries the parameter file defines.
violations: implementing before reading all decisions
steps: Read all design decisions in decisions[] to understand the constraints on tags, budget, and exemptions before proposing any change.

Verify the landing zone exists: the bicepparam file at landing-zones/oases-{env}/oases-{appName}-{env}.bicepparam and the workflow at .github/workflows/lz-oasis-{appName}-{env}.yml — do not proceed if either is missing, this is a create-landing-zone task.
Collect from the user the app/env to manage and which concern to change: cost, tags, or exemptions.
Cost: edit the single `budget` value only. Do not add actual-spend or additional thresholds — the budget drives a single Forecasted alert.

Tags: edit `subscriptionLevelTags` / `resourceLevelTags` keys and placement only. Keep ownerEmail and engineerEmail as readEnvironmentVariable references, never hardcoded values (landing-zone-tags, landing-zone-join-the-platform); keep ownerEmail at subscription level, never resource level.

Exemptions: ask the user whether the exemption is temporary or long-lived. Temporary -> trigger lz-flow-create-policy-exemption (8-hour expiry, break-glass, no PR record). Long-lived -> add an entry to the `exemptions` array referencing a policy-assignment-reference.json key, never a hardcoded ARM assignment id (self-service-policy-exemptions, azure-policy-reference). defenderRecommendationExemptions and diagSettingsExemption remain platform-managed booleans.
Present a complete draft of the bicepparam change before implementing.

For declarative changes (cost, tags, long-lived exemptions) open a PR; the merge triggers lz-oasis-{appName}-{env}.yml, redeploying the landing zone stack with deleteAll. No portal or out-of-band edits (end-to-end-deployment).

nodes.decision: Each application has its own billing scope; every subscription it provisions bills to it.
nodes.why: Without per-application billing scope, cost ownership and autonomy breaks.

links.note: Joining the platform creates the Azure Invoice Section.
Landing zone budget alerts are routed via the action group to the team that owns the spend.

violations: Tags as primary cost attribution instead of invoice section isolation.

nodes.decision: Only Azure Resource Manager (ARM) reachable services are eligible.
nodes.why: If a service isn't manageable through ARM, the platform cannot guarantee service integrity.

links.note: Self-service exemptions are viable because every resource is within Azure Policy's reach — a non-ARM service sits outside Policy's scope entirely.
Security defaults can not be configured for non-Azure services
3rd party services would not be managed by the Azure Deployment Stack.
Non ARM services would require additional logic to collect diagnostic settings

violations: Service adopted with no ARM resource representation — invisible to Policy, RBAC, and diagnostics.

nodes.decision: Every resource type is denied until the platform has validated its security controls, telemetry, and integration pattern
nodes.why: Without an approved list, the platform cannot guarantee that every resource follows its configuration rules.

links.note: Must use an Allowed-prefixed assignment for resource-type compliance.
Every approved type must have a diagnostic settings definition.
New resource type triggers review of whether function-scoped roles need new actions.
Hard deny becomes a bottleneck without a self-service exemption path.
New resource type with no built-in Deny policy requires a custom definition before security coverage is complete.
ARM-reachability is the prerequisite for allowlisting — types outside ARM are ineligible.
Whitelisting triggers a Defender check — paid-SKU recommendations or remediation that requires cost go on the exemption list.
Whitelisting is incomplete until the template has a thin example module

violations: Extending the allowed resources file without reviewing deny policies first.

nodes.decision: When no built-in policy provides a `Deny` or `DeployIfNotExists` effect, the platform authors a custom definition to close the gap
nodes.why: Without custom policies, Audit-only coverage looks like enforcement but the violation still occurs.

links.note: The naming convention constrains custom definitions to Deny, DeployIfNotExists, or Modify effects — the effect determines the assignment prefix.

violations: Resource type whitelisted with a known Deny gap and no custom definition to close it.

nodes.decision: Security controls are enforced through hard deny policies — misconfiguration is blocked, not just reported.
nodes.why: Without enforcement, misconfiguration is invisible.

links.note: All deploy as Deny-prefixed assignments.
Hard denial only works if teams can unblock themselves.
Custom definitions close gaps where built-ins only offer Audit.
A platform-provided public IP exists for teams that need human access to the data plane.
Getting-started modules must comply with active deny policies

violations: Azure resource with public network access enabled.
Resource with local authentication enabled.
Key Vault configured with Access Policies instead of RBAC.
Resource with minimum TLS version below 1.2.
Storage account with cross-tenant object replication enabled.

nodes.decision: Azure Policy assignment names are prefixed by effect — [deny|config|allowed] — followed by the requirement name.
nodes.why: Without a predictable pattern, teams can't find the right assignment without a portal lookup, and self-service breaks down.

links.note: Predictable names are what make exemptions self-service — teams can identify the correct assignment by name, no portal access required.

violations: Effect prefix outside deny, config, or allowed.

nodes.decision: Policy exemptions resolve assignment IDs from a platform-generated reference — never from hardcoded ARM IDs.
nodes.why: Assignment ARM IDs change when a policy is redeployed; a hardcoded ID silently breaks every exemption that references it.

links.note: Stable, predictable names are the keys the reference file is indexed by — they don't change when a policy is redeployed.
The reference file must resolve against the correct management group hierarchy (test or prod) — a script determines which one the subscription belongs to

violations: Manually editing policy-assignment-reference.json.

nodes.decision: No platform change reaches production without passing through the test environment first.
nodes.why: Without an enforced gate, a single push can change production infrastructure with no prior validation.

links.note: Promotion gate targets oases-test for validation.

violations: Workflow deploying to prod from a non-main branch.

nodes.decision: Repo-specific configuration is driven through GitHub variables — no value is set manually.
nodes.why: GitHub environments are destroyed and recreated during BigBang — without variables committed to the repo, the platform state cannot be reproduced.

links.note: New repos receive a subset of platform variables at creation.
Per-environment client IDs sourced from this file.
githubVariables.json must be committed with initial platform config values before BigBang first runs.

violations: Bicep parameter with hardcoded tenant ID or location instead of readEnvironmentVariable().
GitHub variable created in UI.

nodes.decision: Within a deployment stack, what is not declared in code is deleted on the next deployment.
nodes.why: Without this, resources removed from the stack's code persist in Azure — the code stops being the source of truth.

links.note: 'deleteAll' in test verifies code produces the expected Azure state before promotion to prod.
A subscription is guaranteed empty after deleteAll, which makes subscription reuse safe.
Only ARM-native resources are stack-managed — azure-native-services-only is the prerequisite for deleteAll lifecycle enforcement.

violations: Deployment Stack configured with detachAll removed resources persist with no cleanup path.
Resources created directly in Azure are not managed by the Deployment Stack.

nodes.decision: Each platform capability owns its code and deployment pipeline end-to-end, with no shared pipelines crossing capability boundaries.
nodes.why: Without isolation, changing one capability can trigger or break another's functionality.

links.note: deleteAll on each capability's stack ensures no orphaned resources after a declaration is removed.
Main branch is protected by branch policies.
Capability configuration is sourced from the repo.
Each platform capability deploys to the test hierarchy before production.

violations: Resource created or modified directly in Azure portal or CLI outside a Deployment Stack.
Configuration value hardcoded in Bicep instead of injected from GitHub variables.

nodes.decision: Deployment logic is written once in reusable workflows — each environment only controls when and with what inputs to run it.
nodes.why: Without reuse, a fix to deployment logic has to be repeated in every environment workflow.

links.note: Reusable workflows give each platform capability its end-to-end deployment structure.
Gating lives in the trigger layer only.

nodes.decision: The platform has a one-time manual setup process to configure the Azure management group hierarchy and billing, Entra ID authentication, and the GitHub organization.
nodes.why: BigBang can't bootstrap its own prerequisites — it needs identity to authenticate, a hierarchy to deploy into, and a repo to store configuration.

links.note: App registration created, OIDC federation configured, and role assigned at the management group.
GitHub App created and installed.
The subscription-bank management group is created here.
The Gazelle-test and Gazelle-prod management groups are created here.

violations: Automating initial, one-time setup.

nodes.decision: Access is granted by job function, not by resource type — adding a new resource type never requires a new role.
nodes.why: Without job-function scoping, every new allowed resource type forces team re-onboarding.

links.note: Joining the platform creates the Entra ID group that scopes engineer access — roles target the group.
The platform-wide engineers group name (APP_ENGINEERS_GROUP_NAME) is sourced from githubVariables.json
Reader on the 'Subscription Bank' is what allows engineers to discover subscriptions available for reuse.

violations: Custom role with wildcard write permissions.

nodes.decision: Alerts are never hardcoded to a person — they route through the landing zone's action group, which is always sourced from the application profile.
nodes.why: Hardcoded contacts go stale as teams change — alerts fire to former members, incidents go unanswered, and nobody notices until production is affected.

links.note: Application Profile is an authorative source for application contact details.

violations: Budget alert without an action group.

nodes.decision: The platform provides an approved public IP that teams use to open firewall access to their Azure resources.
nodes.why: Without this, application teams cannot access Azure data-plane resources from their laptops.

links.note: The IP is defined in githubVariables.json and propagated as an org variable.
Deny policies are unchanged — firewall configuration is the application team's responsibility.
RBAC roles already grant the authorization — the public IP provides the missing network path.
Getting-started modules use this IP to open and close firewall access.

violations: Platform configuring PaaS firewall rules on behalf of the application team.

nodes.decision: Automation runs inside each landing zone's own subscription, not in a shared platform subscription.
nodes.why: The platform has no management subscription - there is no place to host shared automation runtime.

links.note: Automation jobs query Entra ID, so landing zone identities receive Graph permissions.

violations: Automation job running from a resource outside the landing zone subscription.

nodes.decision: The platform centrally defines what diagnostic settings have to be enabled, but applies them at the landing zone level — telemetry stays local to the application environment.
nodes.why: Without centrally defined diagnostic settings, the platform cannot guarantee that application data is routed to a Log Analytics workspace.

links.note: Must use a Config-prefixed assignment for diagnostic settings.
Diagnostic settings target only Azure native resources.

violations: Resource type added to allowedResources.json without a diagnostic settings definition.

nodes.decision: The platform ships every application repo with a 'hello-world' deployment pipeline, enough to prove it works end-to-end.
nodes.why: Without it, there is no way to verify that a freshly provisioned landing zone is wired correctly end-to-end

links.note: The getting-started modules live in the application repo
Examples use deployment stacks with deleteAll
Examples follow the same trigger/template separation
Examples use the same test-then-prod promotion

violations: getting-started modules tailored for a specific application requirements

nodes.decision: Every landing zone has its own private GitHub runners provisioned inside the landing zone VNet.
nodes.why: Public runners have no network path to PaaS data plane once public access is denied.

links.note: Public runners have no data-plane path once public network access is denied.
Runners get a dedicated /28 subnet, delegated for runner integration, within the landing zone VNet.
GitHub App permissions are used for runner registration and configuration.
Runners provide the network path for automation — human access requires configuring the approved public IP on PaaS firewalls.
PRIVATE_GITHUB_RUNNERS is computed and propagated as an environment variable during landing zone provisioning.
PUBLIC_GITHUB_RUNNERS is defined in githubVariables.json and seeded to application repos at creation.

violations: Application team using a separate VNet for runner integration.

nodes.decision: Each landing zone has a single managed identity — shared across all its workflows, jobs, and service integrations.
nodes.why: Without a single identity, every new capability multiplies the permission surface — RBAC and OIDC config sprawl with no clean audit boundary.

links.note: Automation jobs authenticate as the landing zone's managed identity.

violations: Managed identity shared across multiple landing zones.

nodes.decision: VNet address spaces are allocated by querying the live Azure environment, not by maintaining a registry of what has been assigned.
nodes.why: The platform has no management subscription — there is no place to host ad-hoc tooling like a registry

links.note: Address space is allocated without regional segmentation.

violations: VNet with a manually assigned address space.

nodes.decision: Each application joins the platform once — establishing the Entra ID group, billing scope, and GitHub repo that all its landing zones build on.
nodes.why: Without a registered profile, the platform has no application to provision landing zones for.

links.note: At landing zone creation, the subscription from the bank moves to application's invoice section.
The IDs created at Application Registration (Entra group, invoice section) live as GitHub Variables on the application repo.
The GitHub App is used to create the application repo from the landingzone-template and seeds it with variables.

violations: Landing zone bicepparam with hardcoded owner/engineer email instead of readEnvironmentVariable.

nodes.decision: Landing zone provisioning draws from a subscription bank, returning the subscription to it when the landing zone is sunset.
nodes.why: Azure subscription limit (MCA quota) counts cancelled subscriptions the same as active - it never frees up.

links.note: Reused subscription must move to the application's invoice section before provisioning completes.
Subscription-bank read access is granted through the app engineer role, allowing to discover reusable subscriptions.

violations: New subscription created when bank had an available empty subscription.

nodes.decision: The platform configures a Log Analytics workspace per landing zone, with retention set to stay within the free SKU.
nodes.why: A shared workspace merges ingestion costs across landing zones, which breaks the cost ownership model.

links.note: The cost of diagnostic ingestion lands on the application team's bill.
Diagnostic settings are platform-defined — the platform controls what ingests into this workspace
Log Analytics workspace is regional.

violations: Centralized Log Analytics workspace for general purpose logs.

nodes.decision: Each application has its own GitHub repository — the single control and deployment plane for all its landing zones.
nodes.why: Without a repo, the platform has no deployment mechanism and nowhere to write configuration for the application.

links.note: repository is configured during application registration flow.
The landing zone identity's OIDC federation is scoped to this repo and environment
Each landing zone maps 1:1 to a GitHub environment on this repo.
The GitHub App creates this repo, provisions GitHub environments, and seeds variables — without it, the repo has no provisioning mechanism.

violations: Bring-your-own-Repo for landing zone creation

nodes.decision: All platform-managed resources in a landing zone live in a single dedicated resource group, separate from application resources.
nodes.why: Without a dedicated resource group, there is no clean boundary for teardown, RBAC, or incident management.

links.note: Managed identity is provisioned inside this resource group and managed by Azure Deployment Stack.
Resource group is the Deployment Stack scope — deleteAll tears down every platform-managed resource in one operation.
Log Analytics workspace lives inside this resource group and managed by Azure Deployment Stack.
VNet is provisioned inside this resource group and managed by platform Deployment Stack
Container Apps environment and jobs are provisioned inside this resource group and managed by Azure Deployment Stack

violations: Provisioning a resource outside the main.bicep landing zone template

nodes.decision: Tags are configured automatically across every landing zone, driven by your platform membership.
nodes.why: Without automatic enforcement, tags drift — when an incident fires, alerts point to stale contacts and go unanswered.

links.note: Nightly tag remediation runs as a Container App Job.
Engineer and owner emails come from the platform enrollment.
Tags are enforced via Azure Policy at the subscription level, using a Config-prefixed assignment name.

violations: Introducing a tag that does not apply to all application landing zones

nodes.decision: LEvery landing zone is provisioned from a single shared template, but each has its own dedicated parameter file and workflow.
nodes.why: Without per-zone parameter files and workflows, a team couldn't act on their landing zone without risking triggering another's redeployment.

links.note: Landing zone initial values come from joining the platform.
Each landing zone has a dedicated workflow that passes parameters to the centralized template.
The platform identity is used to create and configure landing zone resources.
The landing zone identity receives Microsoft Graph permissions at landing zone creation time.
landing zone provisioning writes environment scoped variables (resource ID) to the application repo.

violations: Generated workflow name not matching lz-* prefix — breaks fan-out discovery.

nodes.decision: Each landing zone has its own isolated VNet with no connectivity to other landing zones or on-premises networks
nodes.why: A shared networking solution would introduce fixed costs, breaking the no-fixed-cost principle.

links.note: PaaS service firewalls must restrict public network access.
IPAM allocates the VNet's address space, defaulting to a /24.
Each VNet hosts its own runner subnet, registered with GitHub as a dedicated runner group.
The VNet is provisioned in AZURE_DEFAULT_LOCATION; a second region would require a new VNet and a separate regional IPAM allocation.

violations: VNet provisioned outside the landing-zone-resources stack.
Private Link/endpoint configuration on platform managed services.

nodes.decision: Automation images are hosted outside Azure to avoid a fixed cost.
nodes.why: Azure Container Registry carries a flat fee, breaking the no-fixed-cost guarantee.

links.note: GitHub Container Registry is a deliberate exception to azure-native-services-only — its cost profile outweighs the native-services preference here.

violations: Automation job that accumulates cost for a task that could run free.

nodes.decision: The platform authenticates as an Entra ID app registration, not a managed identity
nodes.why: A managed identity requires an Azure resource as its host, but the platform has no subscription of its own — an app registration exists in Entra ID independently of any Azure infrastructure.

links.note: The platform identity holds Directory.ReadWrite.All and Application.ReadWrite.All, used to perform appRoleAssignedTo during landing zone provisioning.

violations: Platform app registration shared with non-platform workloads.

nodes.decision: All platform and landing zone workflows authenticate to GitHub using a single, shared GitHub App identity.
nodes.why: Without a shared GitHub App, the platform has no mechanism to provision and configure application repos and environments.
violations: GITHUB_TOKEN used to write org-level variables or trigger other workflows.

nodes.decision: Landing zone identities receive granular Microsoft Graph read permissions — never broad directory access.
nodes.why: Without these permissions, landing zone automation can't detect Entra ID users, groups and applications.

links.note: Receives User.Read.All, Group.Read.All, and Application.Read.All at landing zone creation time.
Graph permissions are granted via admin consent as a manual prerequisite.

violations: Landing zone identity granted write permissions to Microsoft Graph
Landing zone identity granted Directory.Read.All instead of granular read permissions.

nodes.decision: Every platform change passes through a parallel test environment before reaching production.
nodes.why: Without a parallel hierarchy, platform changes would hit production directly, with failures landing on live landing zones.

links.note: Template workflow changes are validated here first.
The 'Application Registration' workflow is validated here before any platform change reaches production.
New whitelisted resource types are manually tested here before reaching production.
Test has its own dedicated app registration, sourced from a GitHub variable.
Test identity holds the same Graph permissions as production.
Test and production share the same GitHub App.

violations: Application landing zone created in platforms test environment.

nodes.decision: The platform pre-exempts Defender for Cloud recommendations that have no free remediation path
nodes.why: A recommendation with no free remediation path is unactionable noise that erodes trust in the dashboards.

links.note: Defender exemptions follow the same long-lived exemption mechanism — the PR is the review and audit record.
The cost of a Defender for Cloud paid SKU lands on the application's subscription billing.

violations: Defender for Cloud exemption list containing a recommendation with a free remediation path.

nodes.decision: Application teams can exempt themselves from any policy assignment via Pull Request.
nodes.why: The hard deny is only sustainable if teams can unblock themselves — without that, it erodes under operational pressure.

links.note: Exemptions reference a JSON file to look up the policy assignment ID — never a hardcoded ARM ID.

violations: Exemption description that does not name the reason for the exemption.

nodes.decision: All landing zones deploy to a single region — the platform, owns the region decision.
nodes.why: Without a single region, every deployment carries its own region decision — service parity depends on each engineer's choice rather than being a platform default.

links.note: The region is defined once in githubVariables.json — all deployments read it from there.
GitHub runner VNet integration is region-gated, so the platform's chosen region must support it.

violations: bicepparam with hardcoded location overrides workflow-injected region.
Platform region set to a region where GitHub runners VNet integration is unavailable.

links.note: Joining the platform creates the Azure Invoice Section.
Landing zone budget alerts are routed via the action group to the team that owns the spend.

Resource group is the Deployment Stack scope — deleteAll tears down every platform-managed resource in one operation.
Log Analytics workspace lives inside this resource group and managed by Azure Deployment Stack.
VNet is provisioned inside this resource group and managed by platform Deployment Stack
Container Apps environment and jobs are provisioned inside this resource group and managed by Azure Deployment Stack
Nightly tag remediation runs as a Container App Job.
Engineer and owner emails come from the platform enrollment.
Tags are enforced via Azure Policy at the subscription level, using a Config-prefixed assignment name.
Landing zone initial values come from joining the platform.
Each landing zone has a dedicated workflow that passes parameters to the centralized template.
The platform identity is used to create and configure landing zone resources.
The landing zone identity receives Microsoft Graph permissions at landing zone creation time.
landing zone provisioning writes environment scoped variables (resource ID) to the application repo.
PaaS service firewalls must restrict public network access.
IPAM allocates the VNet's address space, defaulting to a /24.
Each VNet hosts its own runner subnet, registered with GitHub as a dedicated runner group.
The VNet is provisioned in AZURE_DEFAULT_LOCATION; a second region would require a new VNet and a separate regional IPAM allocation.
GitHub Container Registry is a deliberate exception to azure-native-services-only — its cost profile outweighs the native-services preference here.
The platform identity holds Directory.ReadWrite.All and Application.ReadWrite.All, used to perform appRoleAssignedTo during landing zone provisioning.
Receives User.Read.All, Group.Read.All, and Application.Read.All at landing zone creation time.
Graph permissions are granted via admin consent as a manual prerequisite.
Template workflow changes are validated here first.
The 'Application Registration' workflow is validated here before any platform change reaches production.
New whitelisted resource types are manually tested here before reaching production.
Test has its own dedicated app registration, sourced from a GitHub variable.
Test identity holds the same Graph permissions as production.
Test and production share the same GitHub App.
Defender exemptions follow the same long-lived exemption mechanism — the PR is the review and audit record.
The cost of a Defender for Cloud paid SKU lands on the application's subscription billing.
Exemptions reference a JSON file to look up the policy assignment ID — never a hardcoded ARM ID.
The region is defined once in githubVariables.json — all deployments read it from there.
GitHub runner VNet integration is region-gated, so the platform's chosen region must support it.
